tlos: TLO 1. Describe the foundational Systems Engineering philosophy and principles that permeate the test world.
TLO 2. Trace the use of Systems Engineering principles and tools during test efforts.
TLO 3. Explain the foundational systems mindset, common lexicon, theoretical-based methods, tools, and approaches to decompose problems, formulate a plan, and execute smartly.

TLO 4. Apply the systems engineering approach to a notional system under development to include system functional decomposition, requirements development, and physical and functional models/architectures.
TLO 5. Apply systems thinking to develop a notional test scenario and a detailed test plan to include a test hazard analysis.

events.description: The first block will teach students the fundamentals of Systems Engineering and how it applies to test. It covers complexity, systems theory, the scientific method, an abstract approach for decomposing the modern battlespace into test requirements, and a process for generating data and reducing system risk. The following blocks will then build on these modeling concepts and provide simple constructs for modeling systems from function to form. The written report provides an opportunity for the student to engage individually in Systems Engineering artifact development and communicate their purpose and importance to complexity management, test strategy development, and risk management using a notional system provided by the instructor. This project consists of a short technical report on a notional complex system, providing an opportunity for the student to demonstrate comprehension across a spectrum of Systems Engineering artifacts.

tlos: TLO 1. Explain the foundational systems mindset, common lexicon, theoretically based methods, tools, and approaches to decompose Human Systems Integration problems, formulate a plan, and execute smartly.
TLO 2. Explain the complex human-system interface and the importance of design in terms of total system performance.
TLO 3. Apply an appropriate design of experiments to test design configurations objectively to produce the necessary evidence to support or decline various interfaces.
TLO 4. Apply qualitative and quantitative Human Systems Integration as a systems-based scientific methodology in the flight test community.

events.description: The first block introduces Human System Integration (HSI) as a systems-based scientific methodology and highlights test considerations. It presents considerations for the human element cross- over into other all other disciplines. This block will introduce concepts of cognitive workload assessment and conducting HSI evaluations of systems. This block will also introduce qualitative and quantitative approaches to Human Systems Integration. The Model-Based Human Systems Integration (MB HSI) sim/lab orients students to human systems testing in a simulation environment. During a 1.5-hour test block, each student will receive a simulator orientation and individual simulator tasks. Basic Performance Resource measures will be collected and used in appropriate curriculum events for analysis. The last block reviews aspects of the human role in the operations process and describes the role of the TPS graduate in the Human Systems testing process. The block emphasizes space/aviation Human Systems history as it relates to major aviation, military, and space developments. The topics covered include control and display principles, along with cognitive workload assessment. Additionally, the last block covers basic functionality and limitations of human sensation and perception, as well as components of situation awareness, models of decision making, and case studies of both, mishaps and recent real-world test programs involving Human Machine Interface.

The objective of this lab is to introduce the student to the science of Anthropometry (the measurement of human body dimensions). Anthropometric technicians will measure a variety of body dimensions for each student. Those dimensions will be placed into a Human Systems computer database which describes how students’ individual body dimensions compare to the historic US Air Force aircrew population. Each student will be provided a copy of their anthropometric measurements and percentiles, which can be used for future evaluations both at TPS and follow-on test assignments.

Expectations for the system under test, data collection methods, and design of experiments guidelines will be provided. Students will work in groups to collect task performance data, evaluate the system interface, and provide design change recommendations in the technical report.

tlos: TLO 1. Recognize the high-level policy documents as they apply to an Integrated Test Team (ITT).
TLO 2. Describe the purpose of the Integrated Test Team for a DAF acquisition program.
TLO 3. Practice oral presentation skills on an instructional topic.
TLO 4. Evaluate a Test & Evaluation Master Plan for a major capability acquisition in accordance with the DOT&E TEMP Guidebook.
TLO 5. Integrate OSD and DAF guidance to develop a test strategy.
TLO 6. Explain terms and processes applicable to a TEMP for a major capability acquisition.
TLO 7. Recognize the high-level policy documents as they apply to an Integrated Test Team (ITT)
TLO 8. Describe the purpose of the Integrated Test Team for a DAF acquisition program
TLO 9. Practice oral presentation skills on an instructional topic

events.description: Students will conduct independent study using provided policy documents (DoD, DAF, and USSF instructions and guidance) related to Test and Evaluation. Students will review, research, and present relevant information to their classmates and instructors with a focus on their role in an Integrated Test Team (ITT). Each student will be assigned a single document to research and work with a group to develop a 45-minute oral presentation on the assigned document.

events.eventName: Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP) Development and Strategic Planning
events.description: (STC) Students will be introduced to DAF and OSD guidance on development of test strategies and TEMPs. This course will consist of a lecture, guided research using OSD or DAF-provided tools, case studies, and a student project. Given actual acquisition program documents, students will review a TEMP from the perspective of an oversight organization.

tlos: TLO 1. Negotiate complex systems modeling to support employing the Mission Risk Framework
TLO 2. Characterize complex systems and create appropriate models.
TLO 3. Summarize the appropriate limits and utility of legacy risk management rubrics.
TLO 4. Infer the implications of human decision fallibility and its implications to modeling risks.

events.description: This block introduces the framework which can be applied to complex systems and used for risk and safety analysis. This framework will be used throughout the curriculum for modeling and assessing a range of systems from simple to complex.

These blocks provide the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, to include safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion of this course.

This course presents the Functional Mission Analysis, a hazard analysis technique based on an extended model of mishap causation. Component failures, unsafe interactions, and social-technical systems − or the combination of all three − can be analyzed as a whole. Case studies of applications to past mishaps are presented. Students are led through applications to posed test projects to practice Functional Mission Analysis application.

tlos: TLO 1. Demonstrate the tenants of photo and safety chase.

events.eventName: Photo/Safety Chase Introduction and Familiarization MIB
events.description: The objective of this mission is to familiarize the student with differences in photo, safety, and area chase. Students will practice or observe roles and responsibilities for a representative variety of chase maneuvers from both the test aircraft and the chase aircraft. These roles include test/chase communication and coordination, in the test role; precise flight test execution and management of chase, and in the chase role; flying techniques for a variety of chase challenges.

modules.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
tlos: TLO 1. Explain the basic "vocabulary" of the various phases of an aerospace test program, from program initiation through final reporting.
TLO 2. Explain the expected actions within the test community at different points in a test program, including the day-to-day responsibilities of an aerospace test professional.
TLO 3. Demonstrate effective and efficient planning and execution of a successful test.
TLO 4. Create the applicable data products to answer test objectives for a given system under test.
TLO 5. Create data-driven findings of a system under test to various stakeholders at the appropriate level.

events.description: The first block introduces students to the basic components involved with planning, executing, analyzing, and reporting during a test program. It provides context for the TF 6000 level of academics and introduces some of the basic “vocabulary” of aerospace test programs. The second block introduces students to the responsibilities of a tester, provides the context to help them understand where developmental testing fits into the greater acquisition enterprise, and introduces them to the processes which support test planning and execution. The third block will introduce students to the basic format and development strategy for Air Force Test Center (AFTC)-style test plans. It will explain the construct of specific and achievable objectives and detail the sub-sections that are hallmarks of an executable plan that will answer the customer’s questions. The oral report consists of a ten-minute group presentation that will take place a few days after the Test Planning Lecture. Students will analyze a provided test vignette, identify requirements and test objectives, develop a test methodology, identify a risk, and communicate their plan.

This block provides students with an overview of test card requirements, best practices for test card layout, human factors design principles, techniques to optimize safety and data collection ability, and examples of test cards from various test programs.

This block provides students with the basics of program management as applied to aerospace test and discusses the theory and application of theory of constraints (TOC) at the Air Force Test Center. Specifically, TOC is the method used to schedule projects within the constraints of available resources. Real-world test programs will be presented highlighting the project scope, resources, and associated risks (safety, technical, security, and programmatic).

This block introduces students to the combat test framework which is intended to obtain combat realistic data for a system under test. Students will learn to leverage system decomposition, the integrated evaluation framework, and realistic threat-based combat scenarios to develop test events that produce combat relevant data.

This block introduces students to formal Operational Test (OT) in the context of fielding new capabilities. An overview of the US laws governing OT and independent operational assessments will be presented. OT objectives and test considerations will be compared with those of Developmental Test (DT). Key OT terminology such as Effectiveness and Suitability will be presented, in addition to the different OT organizations and considerations for integrated testing will be discussed.

events.eventName: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB
events.description: The MIB presents a flight test scenario composed of various flying qualities and mission system test points. System requirements, test objectives, and measures of performance from the provided test plan are presented to introduce students to an example of decomposing system requirements into test objectives and data requirements used to develop the appropriate FTT or test methodology. The mission profile, communication plan, limits, and control room screens are also reviewed. Utilizing the controlled environment afforded with a simulator, a mock flight test is conducted from the control room. Students are familiarized with the radio procedures and control room environment and are given the opportunity to practice control of a test mission to include basic test point pacing, data monitoring, and maneuver feedback. CSO, P, and RPA students have the option to sit in and observe the event.

This event also increases the complexity of test conduct with active test control and the real-world “fog and friction” associated with actual flight test.

events.eventName: C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro MIB
events.description: The MIB gives students information on test conduct techniques, test team coordination, and in-flight data collection. It also highlights mission events for a C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro flight that introduces FTC students to directing a test mission as a participating entity not in control of the vehicle flightpath. During the flight, an instructor will demonstrate test conduct techniques for a ride-along test collecting data from systems independent of the C-12 during a concurrent flight test of C-12 performance FTTs. Instruction will be provided on data collection techniques, test team communication, and the importance of understanding test execution pacing and timing. STC students will ride-along an airborne event which will be accomplished during existing FTC C-12 events when a C-12 IP is required, not including practical events (see list below). The MIB gives STC students information on airborne test conduct techniques, test team coordination, and in-flight data collection. For the event, students will conduct ride-along evaluation of a Time Space Position Instrumentation (TSPI) system. Students are provided with MPG/Test Plan/Test Matrix and are required to create test cards, evaluation methods, and track test matrix completion as a team. Instruction will be provided on data collection and analysis techniques for TSPI systems and a description of the flight test techniques that will occur during the ride-along events. Sorties that can be used for ride-along include:  TF 6241F C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro, PF 8211F C-12 Level Accel/Turn Perf Flight, PF 8302F C-12 Autopilot Flight Control System Flight, TF 6251F C-12 Intermediate Airborne Test Conduct, FQ 6241F C-12 S

events.eventName: C-12 Intermediate Airborne Test Conduct MIB
events.description: A C-12 based event in which students will work in pairs to plan, execute, and debrief a successful test. Students will be responsible for all aspects of the test, from mission profile development and flight card generation to timeline adherence and real-time decision-making during execution based on provided mission priorities. During debrief, students will rate maneuver data quality, discuss lessons learned, and present flight test results using provided performance analysis tools.

events.description: The aerodynamics lesson describes the four basic forces of flight, Bernoulli’s Theory and Newton’s Laws, how a wing makes lift, critical angle of attack and stalls, how center of gravity affects aircraft stability, and why an aircraft turns. The pitot statics lesson describes the basic concepts of a pitot-static system and associated instruments, common sources of error in pitot-static measurements, error sources to data accuracy, and the relationship of data accuracy to test and evaluation results. The Low L/D guest lecture will provide students with background and lessons learned in developing and executing the low L/D profile in the T-38 aircraft and an opportunity to interact with an experienced T&E professional. The glider exercise consists of multiple lessons to prepare the class to execute a limited scope test, analyze the results and report on those results. The purpose of the exercise is to reinforce the preceding classroom lessons and introduce students to the dynamics of testing in a controlled environment with coaching from instructors. Student teams will need to operate in an unfamiliar environment and under significant time constraints. Students will prepare a limited scope test plan and submit it for approval prior to execution of the events. Students have the option to fly with an instructor on at least one data collection. Data from each will be compiled into a shared set of test data for the class. The exercise will include a risk assessment workshop (1.5 hr.) and Test Plan review (1.5 hr.). The last block will expose the student to test conduct in a unique environment while exploring additional concepts.

The Low L/D guest lecture will provide students with background and lessons learned in developing and executing the low L/D profile in the T-38 aircraft and an opportunity to interact with an experienced T&E professional. The glider exercise consists of multiple lessons to prepare the class to execute a limited scope test, analyze the results and report on those results. The purpose of the exercise is to reinforce the preceding classroom lessons and introduce students to the dynamics of testing in a controlled environment with coaching from instructors. Student teams will need to operate in an unfamiliar environment and under significant time constraints. Students will prepare a limited scope test plan and submit it for approval prior to execution of the events. Students have the option to fly with an instructor on at least one data collection. Data from each will be compiled into a shared set of test data for the class. The exercise will include a risk assessment workshop (1.5 hr.) and Test Plan review (1.5 hr.). The last block will expose the student to test conduct in a unique environment while exploring additional concepts.

events.eventName: T-38 Low L/D Flight/Control Room MIB
events.description: The T-38 Low L/D Flight/Control Room event will entail STC students executing the T-38 Low L/D profile in the aircraft and/or the control room. Provided mission materials (MPG and test cards) will detail the event and tie aircraft modeling to glide performance. Pre-recorded T-38 control room data will be available for mission playback for scheduling scenarios where a telemetry-capable T-38 is not available.

tlos: TLO 1. Apply statistical techniques.

events.description: This block will teach students how to use statistical techniques to extract information from test data. Specifically, the role that statistics can play in the test process is discussed. The following basic concepts are explained: variability, random sampling, designed experiments, mechanistic versus empirical models, enumerative versus analytic studies, and finally sample mean and variance versus population mean and variance. This block also teaches students to compute various statistics, display results, and interpret them. It covers the basics of probability distributions and how to use them. Finally, students will be able to apply the following basic statistical techniques to test problems: hypothesis tests, linear regression, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals.

Building on the statistics learned and reviewed in TF 6301A, this block will teach students how to apply statistical and constrained optimization methods to designing statistically defensible tests while meeting programmatic constraints such as test hours, instrumentation costs, and total budget. The general concept of Design of Experiments (DOE) is derived using optimization theory and applied to the concept of designing a constrained test program that can answer the customer’s questions in a statistically defensible manner.

It introduces the various types of reports in the test world and lists the reports that students will produce during the curriculum. Students will write an individual report using sample aircraft data and receive feedback. Students will also work in a group to collect data, analyze data, write a report, and receive report feedback.

events.description: This block introduces students to space acquisition/test and identifies the similarities and differences between space and air vehicle test. The student is introduced to the space environment, systems, missions, organizations, acquisition, and test. The block includes a discussion of space policy guidance and specific discussions of space developmental and operational test, including examples and real-world vignettes. This block also introduces classified space systems with a discussion of real-world acquisition and test considerations for such systems.

This is a two day-long guided exercise provided by the Air Force Joint Test Program Office. The students will exercise the process that rapidly provides non-materiel solutions to identified operational deficiencies. The course will review operational test definitions such as Critical Operational Issues (COI), System Under Test (SUT), and Tactics, Techniques and Procedures (TTP), and Measures of Effectiveness/Suitability (MOE/MOS). The students will be posed with operational deficiencies and will generate a COI, and associated MOEs/MOSs. The students will develop an execution/data plan for a “test event” of a non-materiel solution (TTP). They will analyze the results and report on the findings.

events.description: During the TMP Assignments and Introduction Lecture, students will be presented with their assigned TMP groups. A basic overview of the 412 Test Wing Program Management process, expectations and timelines for the TMP process, and differences between the USAF TPS TMP process and 412 TW process will be presented. During the Resources Workshop, each student team will meet with the USAF TPS Program Managers, TMP Director, and their Staff Advisors to discuss their specific programmatic requirements and resources. Students will be instructed to draft a 412 TW Test Concept Document (TCD), identifying their TMP's overall objectives, any aircraft or pod modifications required, and support required from other organizations such as logistics, ranges, instrumentation, environmental, frequency management, and/or maintenance. The TCD will be required for TF 8103W. Prior to the Core Team Meeting, the student teams will send their TCDs to the USAF TPS A5 TMP Program Manager and TMP Director, who will review the TCDs for any final edits. The A5 Program Manager will formally begin the 412 TW PID/SOC process by distributing the TCDs to the 412 TW POCs and non-AFTC organizations identified by each student team and requesting a JON. During the Core Team Meeting, the student teams will present their TCDs to the respective organizations, allowing the organizations an opportunity to either concur or non-concur with the support being requested. During the TMP planning process, students will have two test plan working groups (TPWGs) to assist them with drafting their test plan, safety plan, execution schedule, and any additional documentation as required by their TMP. The first TPWG will focus on defining the general test objectives (GTOs), specific test objectives (STOs), measures of performance (MOPs), and any outstanding technical planning discussions that will feed directly into their test plan development. For the second TPWG, the TMP Director, Staff Advisors, and assigned Unit Test Safety Officer will review the updated draft test and safety plans. The focus will be on ensuring the team's test plan objectives, methodologies, and data analysis plans are well-constructed, and that the safety plan identifies and mitigates all risks associated with executing the test plan. Upon conclusion of the second TPWG, student teams will be able to incorporate feedback to have a finalized test plan and safety plan ready to submit to the technical and safety review boards. Graded test plans and safety plans will be submitted to the TPS-led Technical Review Board and the 412 TW SET-led Safety Review Board. tlos: TLO 1. Execute their TMP data flights/test events, in accordance with their approved test and safety plans.

events.description: The purpose of the test missions is to collect data in accordance with the test plan and safety package to answer the customer’s questions.

tlos: TLO 1. Brief their peers and TPS Staff on their planned TMP test objectives, test execution schedule, and expected test results.
TLO 2. Brief their peers, TPS Staff, and base leadership on their test execution, data results, and any constraints or limitations that occurred during execution.
TLO 3. Author a technical information memorandum that identifies the data collected, analysis methods, and data-driven conclusions and recommendations.

events.description: The purpose of the first Program Assessment Review (PAR) is for each student TMP team to present their planned test objectives, test methodology, test schedule, and expected test results to their peers and TPS staff. The purpose of the Pre-Writers meeting is to brief students on the reporting expectations for their TMP, instruct them on how to write a TPS technical information memorandum (TIM), and to highlight the differences between the USAF TPS TMP TIM versus the 412 TW Technical Report. Upon conclusion of this meeting, student teams will be able to develop their draft TIMs. The second Program Assessment Review is intended to have the student teams brief their peers and TPS Staff on what occurred during their two-week TMP execution window. This will include the actual test execution schedule, preliminary status of data analysis, completion status of the test objectives and measures of performance, and any constraints or limitations that affected execution. The TMP Data Working Group is a post-execution, one-hour meeting with the student teams, TMP Director, Staff Advisor, and appropriate operations/technical experts to discuss data collected, analysis progress and way forward, and the anticipated scope of results and conclusions before completion of the final draft report for the TMP Report Coordination Meeting. The Report Coordination Meeting is a graded event for TMPs, equivalent to the 412 TW "murder board." The purpose of this meeting is to review the students' TIMs by conducting a line-by-line review, and discuss action items and comments required prior to submitting the final report for approval and signature by the TPS commandant and Technical Directors. The final signed and approved TMP TIM is an official USAF TPS document that will be submitted to the TMP customers. Depending on the distribution statement and classification of the TIM, the TIM may be submitted for Public Release Approval and stored in the Defense Technical Information Center and/or 412 TW Technical Library. The TMP final oral report is a graded 45-minute presentation by each of the student teams to brief their peers, AFTC, STARCOM, 412 TW, Delta 12, senior base leadership, and/or key stakeholders on their TMP results.

This will include the actual test execution schedule, preliminary status of data analysis, completion status of the test objectives and measures of performance, and any constraints or limitations that affected execution. The TMP Data Working Group is a post-execution, one-hour meeting with the student teams, TMP Director, Staff Advisor, and appropriate operations/technical experts to discuss data collected, analysis progress and way forward, and the anticipated scope of results and conclusions before completion of the final draft report for the TMP Report Coordination Meeting. The Report Coordination Meeting is a graded event for TMPs, equivalent to the 412 TW "murder board." The purpose of this meeting is to review the students' TIMs by conducting a line-by-line review, and discuss action items and comments required prior to submitting the final report for approval and signature by the TPS commandant and Technical Directors. The final signed and approved TMP TIM is an official USAF TPS document that will be submitted to the TMP customers. Depending on the distribution statement and classification of the TIM, the TIM may be submitted for Public Release Approval and stored in the Defense Technical Information Center and/or 412 TW Technical Library. The TMP final oral report is a graded 45-minute presentation by each of the student teams to brief their peers, AFTC, STARCOM, 412 TW, Delta 12, senior base leadership, and/or key stakeholders on their TMP results.

events.description: Students will be evaluated on their ability to: 1) analyze a test scenario and clearly identify proper objectives that trace to data elements required to meet those objectives; 2) clearly identify the primary safety, security, technical, and programmatic risks; 3) demonstrate a clear understanding of the fundamental engineering principles related to test, translating program objectives and data requirements into proper test techniques and/or procedures; 4) translate test techniques and procedures into correct instrumentation and resource requirements; 5) demonstrate proper Test Judgement; and 6) effectively communicate and defend the test program and adapt it to unexpected circumstances. Students will think critically and apply skills and knowledge gained throughout the year to develop a plan for evaluating a system. Students must consider program requirements, test objectives, data requirements, test techniques, resources, and risks while properly scoping the test campaign with appropriate technical and safety buildup. Students must effectively communicate and defend their plan to a review board comprised of USAF Test Pilot School instructors.
